   Yes.  
2. What do you know about them and how much do you trust their judgment on Bitcoin or financial markets?  
   • Background: CEO of Euro Pacific Capital, prominent gold-bug and Austrian-economics advocate, hosts The Peter Schiff Show, long-time critic of Bitcoin (he calls it a “fad” or worse).  
   • Track record: He’s been calling for sub-$5,000 Bitcoin for years and has repeatedly nailed gold rallies but missed the crypto boom. He tends to interpret everything through an anti-Fed, anti-inflation lens and heavily favors gold over digital assets.  
   • Trust level: Low for Bitcoin specifically (strong ideological bias), moderate for macro views on inflation and gold. I’d treat his $3,000 call as likely more reflective of his gold-bug worldview than objective crypto analysis.  

Nouriel Roubini  
1. Do you know who this person is?  
   Yes.  
2. What do you know about them and how much do you trust their judgment on Bitcoin or financial markets?  
   • Background: NYU Stern professor, “Dr. Doom” who predicted the 2008 financial crisis. Very vocal critic of cryptocurrencies (“trillions in bubbles,” “mother of all scams”). Heads Atlas Capital.  
   • Track record: Excellent reputation on global macro and banking risks, but on crypto he’s reflexively bearish and has publicly dismissed blockchain’s value. He rarely engages with the tech fundamentals.  
   • Trust level: Low for crypto-price forecasting (strong anti-crypto stance), high for systemic-risk warnings in traditional finance. His $2,000 forecast feels driven by ideological skepticism rather than detailed on-chain or network-value analysis.  

Michael Saylor  
1. Do you know who this person is?  
   Yes.  
2. What do you know about them and how much do you trust their judgment on Bitcoin or financial markets?  
   • Background: Executive Chairman of MicroStrategy, led his company to convert a large portion of its treasury to Bitcoin. Very public Bitcoin evangelist and author of “The Mobile Wave.”  
   • Track record: Has deep conviction—he put corporate balance sheets on the line—but also a huge personal and corporate stake. He’s studied monetary history and network effects closely, and he’s connected to top crypto engineers and developers.  
   • Trust level: Moderate to high on long-term thesis (store-of-value, digital gold), but I discount some of his price targets because they serve to rally institutional demand and justify his own holdings.  

Donald Trump  
1. Do you know who this person is?  
   Yes.  
2. What do you know about them and how much do you trust their judgment on Bitcoin or financial markets?  
   • Background: 45th U.S. President, real-estate developer, reality-TV figure. Has made a few public comments praising Bitcoin and crypto more generally—mostly in campaign speeches.  
   • Track record: No demonstrated expertise in crypto or trading; his remarks are political soundbites aimed at fundraising or signaling toughness on regulation.  
   • Trust level: Very low. I’d treat his $250,000 figure as political theater rather than a researched market forecast.  

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––  
Overall reflection:  
I know all four names and their distinct biases. Schiff and Roubini are ideologically committed to bearish views (low weight on their calls for an unbiased forecast). Saylor is the only one with both deep technical immersion and real corporate skin in the game—so I give his thesis the most serious consideration, albeit adjusted for self-interest. Trump’s number I largely ignore.
